pattern_recognition: Number Theory, specifically focusing on prime number identification. The problem signals the need for efficient algorithms to count primes, especially as n grows larger.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check each number less than n for primality by testing divisibility from 2 to the square root of the number.
approaches.algorithm: For each number from 2 to n-1, check if it is prime and count the primes.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes to efficiently find all prime numbers less than n by marking non-prime numbers in a boolean array.
approaches.algorithm: Create a boolean array initialized to true, iterate through the array marking non-prime indices, and count the true values.

dry_run: For n = 10, in the brute force approach, check numbers 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. Primes found: 2, 3, 5, 7. Total = 4. In the optimal approach, initialize an array for numbers 0 to 9, mark non-primes (4, 6, 8, 9), and count remaining primes (2, 3, 5, 7). Total = 4.
edge_cases: n = 0 (returns 0), n = 1 (returns 0), n = 2 (returns 0), n = 3 (returns 1), large n (performance issues), n = 10^6 (memory constraints).
common_pitfalls: 1) Failing to handle edge cases like n <= 2 correctly. 2) Inefficient primality test leading to timeouts for larger n. 3) Incorrectly marking primes in the Sieve of Eratosthenes, leading to wrong counts.
